title: Income, expenses, and the gap between them
subtitle: Personal finance is simple at its core. Most people make it complicated by ignoring the core.

body.content: Your financial life has one fundamental equation: income minus expenses equals the gap. That gap — positive or negative — is everything. It determines whether you accumulate wealth or deplete it. It is the only number that actually matters in the short term.
Why income is not as simple as your salary

Most people think of income as their take-home pay. But income has a structure. Gross income is what you earn before deductions. Net income is what arrives in your account. The difference — taxes, retirement contributions, insurance — is not wasted. It is a set of financial decisions that someone has already made on your behalf, often ones worth understanding and occasionally ones worth changing.

body.label: Important distinction
body.content: Earned income (salary, wages) is taxed at the highest rates in most jurisdictions. Investment income — dividends, capital gains — is often taxed at lower rates. This is not an accident. It is a policy choice with significant implications for how you think about building wealth over time.

Expenses fall into two categories that matter: fixed and variable. Fixed expenses are committed — rent, loan payments, subscriptions with contracts. Variable expenses are discretionary — they flex with your choices. The distinction matters because your leverage is different in each category. You can cut a variable expense today. Changing a fixed expense takes time, negotiation, or a life change.
The gap between your income and your expenses is the only number that determines your financial trajectory. Everything else is commentary on how to widen it.
